title: BECALMED
byline: Generation ship 'Long Patience'. Reactor cold. No one left but you.

watchroom.title: Watch station
watchroom.text:
You wake in the chair you were not supposed to sleep in. The watch station is dark but for the slow green of a single backup cell, and that green is dying by the minute. No drive-hum under your feet. No air-handler sigh. The ship that has carried four generations toward a star none of you will live to see has stopped carrying anything at all.

The shift log is open on the console. Your name sits at the top of it. The reactor went cold on your watch, and the timestamp says that was nine days ago.

choices.text: Read down the log past your name.
Stand up. Go find the others.

watchroom_log.title: Watch station
watchroom_log.text:
You scroll. The entries after yours are not entries. They are four words, logged by hand, once an hour, by crew who were awake when you were not: he will not wake. Then again. Then a column of it, down and down, in different hands, until the hands stop.

You slept through all of it, in the one chair on the ship where sleep was forbidden, while the cold came in around you like water into a held breath.

choices.text: Close the log. Go find them.

spine.title: Spinal corridor
spine.text:
The corridor runs the length of the ship, a long throat going cold. Frost has started on the rivets, fine as mold. Your breath hangs and does not move; the air is becalmed, as still as the dead drive, going nowhere with you inside it.

Forward lies the bridge, where the watch you failed was kept. Aft, the muster hall, where the others would have gathered. Amidships, the lifeboat lock, where the boat used to be.

sanityText.45: The corridor is longer than it was. You are sure of that now. The frost has crept up the walls in patterns that almost spell four words, and your own breath hangs where you left it, refusing to thin, marking each place you have already stood. Forward: the bridge. Aft: the muster hall. Amidships: the empty lifeboat lock.

choices.text: Sit down here in the cold and stop.

bridge.title: Bridge
bridge.text:
The bridge is a cathedral of dead glass. Every console is dark; the great forward port shows the same handful of stars it has shown for forty years, none of them grown any nearer. The captain's seat faces the dark, and the captain is in it, and has been for nine days, hands folded, waiting out the cold with the patience the ship was named for.

On the helm, under your frost-furred fingers when you reach for it, lies the manual reactor key - the one the watch is meant to turn the instant the core falters. It was an arm's length from your chair. It is an arm's length from you now.

choices.text: Sit in the captain's seat and look at what you let die.
Carry the key down to the reactor deck.

muster.title: Muster hall
muster.text:
The muster hall is where four generations ate and argued and were born. The long tables are set. The meal on them is nine days frozen, untouched, every plate full, as though everyone rose at once and went somewhere better together. No bodies wait here. Only the place that bodies kept, and the cold keeping it.

A wall locker hangs open. Inside, one med-gel ampoule, the field kind, for a mind that has started to come loose. The rest is bare. The others took what they needed and left you this, or left it by accident; you will never know which, and the not-knowing is its own slow weight. Down the length of the tables, the watch-keeper's place is set for one, the chair pulled out. Yours.

choices.text: Walk to your own pulled-out chair and look at it.

lock.title: Lifeboat lock
lock.text:
The lifeboat cradle is empty. The single boat the ship carried, the one mercy for four hundred souls, is gone - launched, days ago, by hands that did the arithmetic and counted you among the lost. The lock cycled and they went, the ones who would fit, out into a dark that will not reach a star in their lifetimes either. They only could not bear to wait in here with the cold and the chair and you.

The outer hatch is still here. Manual. It opens with a wheel a child could turn, onto the same dark they chose. Frost has reached the spokes. Your hand fits them exactly.

choices.text: Press your face to the inner port and watch where the boat went.
Turn the wheel. Follow them out.

reactor.title: Reactor deck
reactor.text: The core is a black slab gone to absolute cold, and the key fits the manual socket the way it was always going to, the way it would have nine days ago if you had been awake to turn it. You turn it now. Nothing catches. The fuel is past restart, the moderators frozen through; the key turns and turns in a dead lock, and the small click it makes is the only sound the ship has left to give you. There was no bringing it back, not after the first hour. You were always only turning a key in the cold to feel your own hand move.

choices.text: Keep turning the key until your fingers stop.

ending.text: You turn it until your fingers stop closing and then go on meaning to turn it after they have. The last green cell on the watch console gives out somewhere far forward. The cold finishes coming in the way it began, patiently, around a held breath, and the ship and the captain and the frozen meal and the empty cradle keep their long patience with you at last.

The wheel gives easily. The dark takes the air in one soft pull and then takes you, and for a single clean moment before the cold there is no chair and no log and no four words, only the same emptiness the others chose, and you go out into it after them the way you should have gone awake into the reactor deck - too late, and willing, and alone.

You sit down in the corridor where the frost spells four words and you do not get up. Nowhere the getting up would take you. The ship holds still around you, becalmed, going nowhere, and you keep its last watch the way you kept its first, sitting down, until the cold closes the log on you too and the green cell forward winks out and the ship is finally, fully, alone.
